conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the eligibility rules so I can ask you the right questions.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I've reviewed the eligibility rules. To help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have two children - Alex, who's 8, and Blake, who's 14.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have Alex (8 years old) and Blake (14 years old).

Do Alex and Blake currently live with you in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live with me here in the UK. We're all together at home in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great, thank you. Now I need to ask: is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital, care, or residential accommodation for an extended period?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in hospital or care or anything like that. They're both at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you. One final question: do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000 per year? 

(This won't affect your eligibility to receive Child Benefit, but it may mean you need to pay a tax charge on it.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I don't have information about that in my situation. I'm just trying to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit in the first place.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's absolutely fine! The income question relates to a potential tax charge, but it doesn't affect your actual eligibility to receive Child Benefit.

Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough to assess your eligibility. Let me provide you with my decision:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for both of your children.**

**For Alex (8 years old):** You are eligible because Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for them, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

**For Blake (14 years old):** You are eligible because Blake is under 16, lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for them, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

You can claim Child Benefit for both children. Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, so as long as no one else claims for Alex or Blake, you should be able to receive the benefit for both of them.

Please note that when Blake turns 16, you can still continue to receive Child Benefit if they stay in approved education or training until they're under 20 years old.

If you'd like to proceed with your application, you can apply for Child Benefit through the official government channels. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
